lens.included_activities: United States lower-middle-market publishers that repeatedly collect, verify, organize, update, license, rent, or publish directories, mailing lists, databases, and other factual compilations for external advertisers, subscribers, marketers, professional users, or institutions in print or electronic form.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal customer files, one-off compilations without repeat external customers, search and social platforms classified elsewhere, advertising agencies and direct-mail fulfillment firms without publishing activity, retailers of third-party directories, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue can come from subscriptions, access and download fees, sale or rental of standardized or customer-specified lists, data licenses, advertising in directories, and recurring updates or data services. Customers pay for audience access, structured factual coverage, data freshness, searchability, and compliance-sensitive list management.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 513100 publishing group rather than directory and mailing-list publishers and reports business and financial operations at 10.68 percent of employment, computer and mathematical occupations at 8.71 percent, arts and media at 29.00 percent, sales at 13.37 percent, and office and administrative support at 16.13 percent. Those information-heavy occupations are mapped to source discovery, extraction, entity matching, deduplication, classification, description generation, verification queues, list customization, marketing, sales support, and customer service. Directory firms may have more structured data work and less judgment-heavy editorial work than the parent group, so the parent mix may understate task exposure; field verification, relationship sales, compliance, and final data accountability push the other way.
a.rationale: Much of the current service basket consists of repetitive data acquisition, cleaning, matching, enrichment, classification, formatting, search, list assembly, update detection, marketing, and support. Human work remains important for source rights, exception resolution, sensitive-data review, quality assurance, customer interpretation, relationship sales, and accountability for harmful errors.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted time study separating data acquisition, verification, matching, enrichment, engineering, product, sales, customer success, privacy, compliance, and print production, net of automation already deployed, would materially change the range.
caveats: The occupation mix covers newspapers, periodicals, books, and directories rather than 513140 alone.
Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and do not reveal current database automation.
The surviving industry may already be more automated than the broader historical category.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures business-level AI use across the two-digit Information sector, not implemented substitution of exposed directory-publishing task-hours. As of May 3, 2026, 39.7 percent of Information businesses reported current AI use and about 42 percent expected use within six months. Structured digital records, APIs, recurring update cycles, and measurable match quality support above-current adoption over five years, while source licensing, privacy, deletion and suppression duties, hallucination, provenance, cybersecurity, and legacy systems reduce realized substitution.
rho.rationale: AI can be embedded into existing collection, entity-resolution, enrichment, quality-control, query, and sales workflows, and output quality is measurable. Implementation still requires data rights, monitoring, human exception handling, audit trails, security, suppression logic, and client-specific integration.
rho.change_evidence: Matched pre/post evidence from 513140 firms on AI workflow coverage, records processed per labor hour, match precision and recall, false-positive harm, deletion compliance, data-source cost, customer retention, cloud and model expense, and avoided hiring would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The AI-use evidence is for the broad Information sector and any business function.
Adoption does not measure production depth or labor substitution.
Privacy and consent requirements vary by data type, customer, state, and communication channel.

e.rationale: The official category includes online and print directories, mailing lists, databases, and factual compilations. Paid subscriptions, downloads, recurring licenses, list rentals, advertising programs, and update services can fit the outsourced-service lens, while legacy ad-supported consumer directories, one-off list sales, captive data, public-record repackaging with weak differentiation, and nonrecurring print products do not.
e.change_evidence: A verified 513140 LMM register connecting product mix, subscription and repeat-license revenue, customer concentration, renewal rates, proprietary-source rights, advertising dependence, normalized EBITDA, privacy obligations, and transferable systems would materially change eligibility.
caveats: No direct audit of the missing LMM-band firm population was found.
The category mixes subscription data products, mailing-list rentals, ad-funded directories, and legacy print products.
The frozen compensation input is an ancestor-sector proxy, and the dataset provides no defensible n value.

s5.rationale: Recurring subscriptions, proprietary source relationships, taxonomies, longitudinal records, and niche customer access can attract strategic data, software, marketing, or information buyers. Qualifying transfers are constrained by missing population and deal-flow data, legacy decline, uncertain data rights, privacy liabilities, source dependence, customer concentration, and the risk that technology replaces rather than acquires the operator.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of US 513140 control transfers from PitchBook, CapIQ, company announcements, state filings, and broker records, matched to an eligible LMM denominator and including asset purchases, failed sales, data-rights diligence, and customer retention, would materially change the range.
caveats: No six-digit transaction rate or defensible eligible-firm denominator was found.
Data and list assets may sell without transferring a continuing operating firm.
Privacy liabilities and nonassignable source contracts can impair transferability.

q.rationale: Annual subscriptions, access fees, list rentals, data licenses, and fixed advertising packages can preserve efficiency between renewals, especially where proprietary coverage and workflow integration create switching costs. Retention is reduced by renewal repricing, usage-based contracts, customer procurement, commoditized public data, quality concessions, source-licensing cost, cloud and AI expense, privacy operations, deletion duties, and competition from self-service databases and software.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level panels connecting revenue model, renewal timing, price change, usage commitments, source cost, AI and compliance cost, records per labor hour, data quality, churn, and gross margin would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No direct study measures post-AI benefit retention in 513140.
Pricing power differs sharply between proprietary professional data and commoditized contact lists.
Compliance and deletion costs may consume efficiency rather than pass through cleanly.

d5.bridge: BLS reports directory and mailing-list publisher employment fell 75.1 percent from 58,849 in 2000 to 14,671 in 2024. Employment is translated into five-year constant-quality demand only as a structural-decline proxy: continued substitution of print directories and simple lists pushes demand downward, but historic employment also reflects productivity and reclassification, while proprietary databases, B2B prospecting, compliance screening, and online subscriptions may support surviving service volume. Because employment is not output, the five-year decline range is far milder than the historical employment contraction.
d5.rationale: Printed directories and undifferentiated lists face continued displacement by search, platforms, customer-owned data, direct APIs, and AI discovery. Demand persists for verified niche data, proprietary professional compilations, compliant audience access, enrichment, historical coverage, and integration into customer workflows.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast using inflation-adjusted 513140 product revenue, paid subscriptions, licensed records, list-rental volumes, renewal cohorts, print circulation, digital query volume, active establishments, and substitution by search, data platforms, and customer-built tools would materially change the multiplier.
caveats: A long-run employment decline is not a direct measure of constant-quality service demand.
The 2000-2024 period spans digitization, productivity change, reclassification, and business exits.
Fast-growing proprietary databases may diverge sharply from legacy print directories and commodity mailing lists.

o.rationale: Customers can increasingly search public sources, enrich their own CRM data, buy API access, and use software or AI to assemble and update simple directories and lists. An accountable operator remains useful where value depends on licensed or proprietary sources, entity resolution, provenance, verification, suppression and deletion, sensitive-data controls, auditability, specialist taxonomy, service levels, and liability-bearing customer support.
o.change_evidence: Cohort evidence comparing operator-managed products with customer-built, platform, and AI-only alternatives on coverage, precision, freshness, opt-out compliance, harmful errors, acquisition cost, conversion, customer retention, and total cost would materially change the operator-required share.
caveats: No direct substitution study isolates NAICS 513140.
Compliance requirements preserve accountable work but can also make products uneconomic.
Operator need is much lower for public-record aggregation and simple lists than for proprietary regulated datasets.

narrative.executive_view: Directory and mailing-list publishing combines unusually automatable structured-data work with recurring subscription, licensing, rental, and advertising models, but the category has experienced severe long-run contraction and faces direct substitution by search, software, APIs, and customer self-service.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can automate source discovery, extraction, matching, deduplication, classification, enrichment, descriptions, verification queues, search, list customization, marketing, and routine support. Source rights, privacy, deletion and suppression, sensitive-data review, exception resolution, relationship sales, and final quality accountability remain operator work.
narrative.value_capture: Subscription and license terms can preserve gains between renewals, particularly for proprietary niche data, but customers can demand lower prices or more coverage and the operator must fund data sources, cloud systems, AI assurance, security, and expanding compliance operations.
narrative.firm_availability: Niche taxonomies, longitudinal databases, source relationships, recurring customers, and workflow integrations can transfer to strategic buyers. Availability is unmeasured and weakened by missing LMM counts, historical exits, privacy liabilities, customer concentration, source-contract restrictions, and asset-only transactions.
narrative.demand_durability: Legacy print directories and commodity lists remain under structural pressure, while verified professional data, compliant audience access, enrichment, and embedded workflow products can persist. The result is a broad but contraction-centered five-year demand range.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest risks are missing firm counts, broad ancestor labor data, a heterogeneous product mix, continued search and software substitution, public-data commoditization, privacy and deletion mandates, sensitive-data enforcement, source licensing, cybersecurity, model hallucination, and declining economics in legacy print products.
narrative.principal_driver: Structured, repetitive data collection, matching, enrichment, publishing, marketing, and support workflows provide deep AI exposure and recurring service opportunities.
narrative.principal_weakness: Long-run industry contraction and direct self-service substitution threaten the quantity of work that remains with an accountable directory or list operator.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 513140 Directory and Mailing List Publishers
sources.supports: Official definition covering directories, mailing lists, factual compilations, public records, and print or electronic publication.

sources.title: Census NAPCS Product List for Directory and Mailing List Publishers
sources.supports: Product definitions for standardized and customer-specified mailing lists sold or rented and online directories funded by subscriptions, downloads, or other fees.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: NAICS 513100 Newspaper, Periodical, Book, and Directory Publishers
sources.supports: Broader publishing-group occupation employment and wage mix, including data, technology, editorial, sales, administrative, and production work.

sources.title: Census BTOS: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Current and expected AI use by US businesses, firm size, and sector, including Information-sector adoption and implementation context.

sources.title: BLS: Industries with Employment Decreases from 2000 to 2024
sources.supports: Long-run employment contraction for directory and mailing-list publishers, including 2000 and 2024 employment levels and percentage change.

sources.title: FTC CAN-SPAM Act Compliance Guide for Business
sources.supports: Commercial-email obligations, opt-out processing, restrictions on selling or transferring opted-out addresses, and retained legal responsibility.

sources.title: California Privacy Protection Agency: Information for Data Brokers
sources.supports: Data-broker registration and 2026 deletion-platform obligations, including recurring access and processing of deletion requests.
